Text: The author of the communication (initial submission dated 10 February 1988 and subsequent correspondence) is C. G., a Jamaican citisen currentiy awaiting execution at St. Catherine District Prison, Jamaica. He is represented by counsel.

The author was arrested on 7 April 1934 and charged with the murder, together with a co-defendant, N. D., of one A. I., in the District of Manchester, Jamaica; he claims to be innocent. On 11 October 1984, he was sentenced to death in the Westmoreland District Court; his co-defendant was convicted of manslaughter and sentenced to 30 years imprisonment (reduced to 20 years on appea)). The Court of Appeal of Jamaica dismissed his appeal on 28 July 1987.

As to the facts of the case, a/ it is submitted that tie ul)... broke intc Mr. I.'s house at dawn, together with three other men, allegec!y w ch the intention of stealing money. Mr. I. and his femily (his wife and two daughters) were threatened with death and forced to hand over all their money. according to Mrs. I.'s account, her husband was shot during the robbery. One of his daughters, L. I., also teatified that C. G. allegedly had admitted to her that he had shot her father. During the identification parade on 11 May 1984, L. I. purported to identify the author as the murderer. In this connection, the author claims that the police officers who conduct. the parade influenced the deceased's widow and daughter as to whom they were to identify. He further points out that the deceased's widow failed to identify him.

The author states that the conduct of the identification parade was challenged unsuccessfully by his legal aid attorney during the trial. He further claims that he had no opportunity to consult with his lawyer prior to or during the trial and appeal proceedings.

By decision of 21 March 1988, the Working Group of the Human Rights Committee * anemitted the communication to the State party and requested it, under rule 91 of the rules of procedure, to provide information and observations relevant to the question of the admissibility of the communication. It further requested the State party, under rule 86 of the rules of procedure, not to carry out the deah sentence against the author while hie communication was under consideration by the Committee.

In hie submission under rule 91, deted 25 October 1988, the State party argues that the communication is inadmissible purauant to article 5, paragraph 2 (4), of the Optional Protocol because the author may still apply, for epecial leave to appeal to the Judicial Committee of the Privy Council. It further esubmite that legal aid will be available for that purpose.

In his comments, dated 28 December 1988, author's connsel argues that the sole iaaue in the caoe relates to the treatment of identification evidence. He challenges the author's identification by the deceased's daughter and seiterates that the deceased's widow did not identify the author. While conceding that the case does not fall into the category of fleeting qlance identification, counsel contends that the nature of the identification by the deceaned's Jaughter called for a careful and precise summing up by the judge, given the absence of corroborative or other suppurting evidence. He further submits that the judge did not comply with the strict rules on ideatification guidelines laid down by the Court of Appeal of England in the case of R. vy. Turnbul) (1976) b/ and that, as a result, he misdirected the jury on several relevant issues. In particular, the judge is,aaid to have failed to warn the jury that a mistaken witness may be a credible witness) that he misdirected the jury as to the lack of corroborative or other supporting evidence for the author's identification by tha deceased's daughter; that ne inadequately directed the jury in relation to identification on the conditions prevailing during the robbery in the middle of the night; that he wrongly inferred that support for the identification by the deceased's daughter could come from identification evidence of N. D.

Before considering any claims contained in a communication the Human Rights Committee must, in accordance with rule 87 of its rules of procedure, docide whether or not it is admissible under the Optional Protocol to the Covenant.

The Committee has ascertained as it is required to do under article 5, paragraph 2 (a), of the Optional Protocol, that the matter ie not being examinsd under another procedure of international investigation or settlement.

With respect to the requirement of exhaustion of demestic remedies, the Committee has noted the State party's contention that the communication is inadmissible under article 5, paragraph 2 (b), of the Optional Protocoi, since the author may still petition the Judicial Committee of the Privy Council. It observes that the author has obtained legal representation for this purpose, and that his counsel in London is currently preparing a petition for special leave to appeal to the Judicial Committee of the Privy Council on hie behalf. It canavt conclude, on the basis of the information before it, that a petition for spevial leave to appeal to the Judicial Committee of the Privy Council would not constitute an effective remedy available to the author within the meaning of article 5, paragraph z (b), of the Optional Protocol.

‘The Human Rights Committee therefore decides: (a) That the communication is inadmissible under article 5, paragraph 2 (b), of the Optional Protocol) (b) That, since this decision may be reviewed under rule 92, paragraph 2, of the Committee's rules of procedure upon receipt of a written request by or on behalf of the author containing information to the effect that the reasons for inadmiseibility no longer apply, the State party shall be requested, taking into account the spirit and purpose of rule 86 of the Committee's rules of procedure, not to carry out the death sentence against the author, before he hag had a reasonable time, after completing the effective domestic remedies available to him, to request the Committee to review the present decision; (c) That this decision shall be transmitted to the State party, to the author and to his counsel. Notes B/ The author's submissions do not provide a detailed account of the facts. The following description follows the outline of the facts in the judgment of the Court of Appeal. b/ See 63 Cr. App. R 132. These guidelines are also applied by Jamaican courts. Subsequent to counsel's comments, the Judicial Committee of the Privy Council allowed the appeal of Oliver Whylie and quashed the judgment of the Court of Appeal in hie case. In its judgment of 27 July 1989, the Judicial Committee of the Privy Council stated that Their Lordships have no hesitation in concluding that a significant failure to follow the guidelines laid down in Turnbul] will cause the conviction to be quashed because it will have resulted in a substantial miscarriage of justice.
